# Sheila Simsarian Webber (Innovative Teaming)

<!-- vimeo: 1111453548 | track: English (auto-generated) -->

Hi there. I'm Sheila Weber, professor of Management, and I'm really excited to share with you how I've been thinking about and using AI to improve team dynamics for student project teams. Um, this is a specific example from a first year course in our business school called Innovative Teaming that I work on with my colleague Katerina Gonzalez at Suffolk University in Boston. So the goal here was to really think about how could AI give teams feedback on their teamwork to improve their team dynamics to, to accomplish that. We had students, um, have a team meeting, a virtual team meeting using Zoom with the AI companion selected so that they would get the AI companion meeting notes and student teams followed a specific agenda for this meeting. And then at the end of the meeting, they would get the AI companion meeting notes through Zoom. Step two involved the teams then taking their AI companion meeting notes and then using AI to evaluate their team dynamics and give them specific feedback about what went real, what went well for the team, and what are the areas for improvement, and then actual action steps. So I've created a GPT to analyze these meeting notes and I'll show you how that works. So here is the, um, the team coach meeting analyzer, and students simply can select one of the options there, which basically just ask them to provide their Zoom AI companion meeting notes. They can paste those meeting notes right in to the chat box here and input those in. And then, um, the meeting analyzer will provide them with specific EV information about their team's strengths, uh, by, based on research and things that we know are important for teams to work effectively together. And then their specific areas for improvement. Again, um, important areas that are identified for the team to improve. And then it will provide them with actual actionable steps for improvement. So what we had teams do at this point is to come back to class and to use the feedback from AI to actually have a meeting during class time to take a look at that feedback. And so when teams were then back in class, take a look at their team dynamic analysis that the AI generated, um, critically think about it, what is it about that that actually resonates for them? What did they like about it? Um, what did they think was important in terms of the feedback that they got? And engage in a discussion about that to then identify clear specific action steps that they can use right away to improve their team. And we did this with the teams about a two weeks before their final papers and presentations are due so that they had time to make some team work improvements. Um, this also allowed us to engage in a really interesting conversation with the students about the discussion of AI as a teammate and how the data that's being collected in organizations can be used really to enhance and elevate your team dynamics, but also might hinder your team dynamics if you think that, um, you are being monitored and, and information is being tracked and used in certain ways. And you can see, uh, this information and read more about this in an article that I wrote in organizational dynamics about the paradox of artificial intelligence as a teammate. So overall, students really enjoyed this. They were very surprised by how well AI can analyze their meeting, can understand team dynamics, and could give them really actionable steps to take. And again, these were first year students with not a, not a lot of, uh, teamwork experience. And so this exercise could be done in any class where you are using a team and you want the teams to engage in some discussion about their team dynamics. It's a really great way to help people to have the language, to have those important conversations about their strengths and areas for improvement. So thank you so much and if you have anything you'd like to learn more about or interested in this, um, topic, please reach out to me. Here is my email. Thank you.
